speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6 of the survivors of the Shoah interview with Itka Zygmuntowicz, March 3, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to continue the story, so when we went to work, what made it harder was that, first of all, it was, at that point, was not useful work. Because we could carry the same stone back and forth just to wear us out, to mock us, and to, to, you know, to have-- give them a chance-- their sadism to come up. Secondly, if one fell or whatever, we couldn't-- we're not allowed to help each other openly. We did it in, in hiding, but not openly. We could not speak on, on the-- so we had hardly any control. For instance, like, we didn't have water to wash. So we used to go, just to feel the-- go through the motions sometimes in the morning. I used to do that because I was used every morning to wash, you know, [INAUDIBLE]. And still would say, in the morning, the prayers, you know. And at night, even I had no longer my family to say it with me. But to try as much as I could, you know. So physically, I was totally enslaved. I had no control of life. Spiritually, I could think and feel what I wanted. And I remember one time when I was in Auschwitz, and I felt the burden, the bitter taste of slavery. And I felt, if I would have a pencil and paper now, I would write a poem. But there was no pencil and paper. I told you, my earthly possessions were what I wore and the bowl, the enamel bowl from which we got the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote a poem in my, in my head. And when I was liberated, when I came here to America, of course, I couldn't speak a word of English. It was constant adjusting from one to the other. So that was among my first poems, and I would like to share it.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'I feel like a bird with clipped wings tied to this earth by invisible strings, chained to a destiny I did not choose. I feel like a prisoner that cannot break loose. I look at the sky with a heavy sigh, but my wings have been clipped, and I can no longer fly.' Then I realized that the concept of freedom is a bird in flight and not in a bird in a cage. And I pledged to myself, if I'll get out, I will never use brute force. I will never try to force somebody to do something, but neither will I allow other people to do it. I understood the concept of freedom. I understood what my forefathers, what the, the Jews in Egypt, the Israelites in Egypt, must have felt like. And I realized that there is no substitute for personal experience, from knowledge derived from personal experience. I realized then that nothing in the world, no textbook, no professor, not the best college, could teach me what my experience taught me. Because I had to-- I got to know myself, who I am, how much I could endure, how much I could understand, how much I could feel, and what I've became, and saw the world through a completely different things. Because I was thinking, Mengele was a university-educated man with doctoral degrees. He had the ability to heal the, the sick, and perversely, he choosed to, to kill the, the healthy imprisoned in Auschwitz, more than 400,000 Jews, [NON-ENGLISH], were murdered because of him, the selections, to the left, to the right. And I realized the power of words, how words can heal and, and kill, how it is. And I started-- everything was the reverse. It, it was, it was amazing. And all the things that I'd been taught all my life and everything. And then, this same Mengele [INAUDIBLE] was such a sadist. He would take, make, you know, select people and would make operations with people. And some men were castrated. I personally know somebody who lives in Israel who will never, never had a family because he was castrated. Some didn't survive experiments without, without, without anesthesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But this I was told by people. If I would have seen, then I would have been one of them. Same like with the gas chambers. If I would have seen the inside of gas chambers, I wouldn't be here to tell you. But how do I know? Because what I found out later. I didn't know at the time. You see what the Nazis did, is that they selected, they selected people. It was called the Sonderkommando, men who work there. And they, after three months, they know that they should not, if they survive, they shouldn't survive. They shouldn't tell what's happened. They wanted to destroy, not to have witness. They would gas them, too. And when people would come, including my family, they would send them straight to the gas chamber. But in order that they should not rebel, that they should not, they should go, they told them that they're going to take a bath. Now, some people came. They didn't have a bath for a long time. So they were glad. And they gave them a piece of soap and a towel, and on the door-- because they-- some [INAUDIBLE] will survive. And they told later the stories. And they took them in, and instead for gas for water, with [INAUDIBLE] gas. And then, everything that was ours became theirs. They, they gassed them. Later, there were barbers that cut their hair, and that was made to mattress stuffing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, were you there at all for any of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, because I would be dead, David. I wouldn't be here to tell you, to see it. Fortunately, I didn't go in. But that's how my family and all the others. And there was some-- well, there is a story that somebody's written that one of them who worked there, he, he was there first. And then he recognized somebody from his family. And he couldn't go through it, so he committed suicide because he knew they going to kill him. Now, remember, I mentioned to you before about Roza Robota earlier. Roza Robota and others were able to organize. They were in the underground, and they are organized. And they tore up one of the crematoriums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her at all in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, I didn't see her. No. But this is well known because there were other people from my city. Plus, I was in the museum where it's written up, and I have some literature about this. So this is a story which there are eyewitnesses to. So I didn't see her. I saw another hanging, but I didn't see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they caught her. And they brought her back. And before they hung her, they tortured her. They put her in solitary. They tortured her to tell who are the others, and she would not betray. So they hung her in front of everybody to teach us all a lesson. They always did that. When there was a hanging, we all had to stay and watch. And her last words, according to what I heard, is [NON-ENGLISH]. Be brave and strong. And that's how she died. Can you tell us about the hanging that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hanging that I saw was from a girl that was called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], something with a Z. I don't remember the exact name. But she was a, a carrier, a messenger. She was a very beautiful girl. I saw her. I didn't know her personal like a friend or anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was born, from what I'm told, in Poland. But she came from Czechoslovakia, how it came. And she ran away. And they caught her. They brought her back. Nobody could run very far because first of all, of the way we looked, our condition. And for months, they evacuate everybody around Auschwitz. And where would she run? Nobody would take her in, so-- but she run away, nevertheless.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they-- one day they asked us all to stand at appell, and I saw a gallow. And they were going to hang her. And all of a sudden she had hidden someplace a razor. And with my own eyes, I saw her cutting the wrists. And with the hand that was bloody, still alive, you know, like this kind of motion. I don't remember whether she slapped him exactly-- and anyhow, that's how she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were-- some died-- not everybody died the same deaths. Some died of starvation. Some died because they were shot. Other dies of illnesses. Some died in the gas chambers. Did you see people get shot in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I [INAUDIBLE] people, personally? No, I don't remember seeing anybody shot in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people died all kinds of different deaths, and they experienced it differently because they came as different people. And they made the experiments. And usually, to lure people, I remember one time, I was very suspicious when they said that they're going to give you a little more bread. I'd already experienced they give a little bit more when we came from the ghetto. So I never volunteered for anything. So one time, they came and they were trying to-- how you call?-- recruit girls to take for those experiment that they took to Auschwitz. So they also promised more bread. I didn't volunteer. Bina and I was trying to hide. We couldn't run away, but we tried to be inside. And didn't volunteer anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I later found out after the war, after the Holocaust, we met some people who survived those experiments. Some, as a result, will never bear children. Others were injured in different way. One woman was-- she is unfortunately no longer alive, may she rest in peace. Her, on her face was growing hair like a man. I don't know what they did to her. So she had to have, like, special-- electric kinds of treatment, you know. There were all kinds of different-- And also, and we-- it was like the late Professor [NON-ENGLISH] from [INAUDIBLE] College calls 'A planet of death.' A place where no-- just women, no children. No-- you never heard children play or laugh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see children in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the language different?

speaker: interviewee
text: The language there? Some people, I couldn't speak at all because we couldn't speak the language. So if I could speak to somebody who spoke Yiddish or Polish, I spoke. I know a little bit Hebrew, but nothing that I would call it-- to me, knowing a language is when you can read and write. And you can understand yourself, have a conversation. So it was very difficult [INAUDIBLE]. There wasn't too much time to talk. And you didn't feel like talking much because we were-- at work we couldn't. We came home, we were dead tired. And we came from work when we marched in, again, standing for hours, sometimes, till they dealt out the food. And there was also something about the food. We got so little food. We were so starved. So there was, like I mentioned before, a unwritten law that you would not take one survivor from the other wouldn't take. With Esther was the only incident in all the years. But certain people, when they were carrying the cauldrons of, of soup, they would lift the top and put in their bowl-- some had a bowl, others had a cup-- and run. If you were caught, got 25 on your bare behind. So I didn't want to risk it. And so the starvation, the starvation was, was just terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far as thinking, when I started to think about home, I started to cry. When I started to think about the future, I know there was no future. So you didn't, you didn't think too much. I just do stories. I tried just, sometimes in spite of myself, I try to remember the things that my parents taught me. And I always before I did something, till this day, I always said, what would my grandmother and parents say to that? I like-- it's not so much seeking their approval, but I tested their values, and they work for me. Did you have any knowledge of the outside world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- I didn't. And I want you to know something. The things that we take for granted, in all the time from November 22, 1942, till liberation, 1945, I never brushed my teeth. I never saw myself in a mirror. I never knew what date, what time is, what anything is. Completely-- I had a very rich inner life from-- I was always an avid reader. Even as a young child, I was reading a lot, you know. And ironically, I used always to go back sometimes to remember certain things. When I was in school, my favorite-- least favorite subject was history. Because it was taught in such a way, a bunch of dates and places not showing with the relevance to our life. And I didn't know then, but I know now that history is made by people, and it affects people life. I started to realize that what I say and do to you now will affect both you and I, and that every deed that we do affects us all. I wrote a [INAUDIBLE] that occurred in my mind, how important choices are. I know people by the choices they make. So whatever we say, we choose to say and do, we are becoming partners to. But others are becoming the beneficiaries of our good deeds or the victims of our evil deeds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, I started to understand what my grandmother and parents taught me. [NON-ENGLISH]-- good deeds, this is the most important. That was, you know, always important, good deeds. [NON-ENGLISH], did you do right? Did you do wrong? You know, I don't remember being ever physically spanked. It was enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember I told you a story about-- I started to talk, I remember, about my father's story. In Auschwitz, I thought a lot about that story. When I was in Poland yet, before the Holocaust, before means till the age of 13, during from 13 till 19, and then after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I went to school, it was cold in Poland so I was wearing wool stockings. Till this day, I don't know how we held them up. But they always had like bagels, you know, they were always rolling, you know. They didn't stay. They didn't stay up straight. And some of the girls started to wear knee socks. So I wanted to be like the other girls, too. So one day I ask-- my parents, who were there, I said, can I put on my knee socks? My parents said, Itkalo, it's too cold. Your knees going to freeze. So at that time, I was a naughty girl. And I figured, why do I have to convince them, which was wrong, what I did. I realize it now, and I regret it. So what I did, I didn't say anything. I went into the other room, put on the knee socks under the stockings, walked out in the stockings and went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my father, as I mentioned before, used to work in photography and picture framing. The picture framing he did in the house, the other work in Warsaw. So often, he would-- often, he would-- well, he would come from, from a business trip. He used to stop by. It wasn't far, the school, from where we lived. He'd stop by, bring something, or just say hello, or when he had to leave, you know, in a hurry without saying-- he used to come often. At that time, I used to be embarrassed. They said, Itkalo, your father. But now I am so grateful that he was so thoughtful and so loving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this particular time, it's recess, and somebody says to me, Itkalo, your father is here. It was too late to do something, anything about the knee stocking. I thought that-- I cannot tell you how I felt. My father looked at me, and he said to me, Itkalo, I feel very hurt and disappointed. He said, you know what? Everybody makes a mistake. But when you lie, that means deliberately you want to cheat somebody. But remember, you can never cheat others without cheating yourself. The biggest cheater is somebody who cheats himself. He says, I trusted you. You betrayed my trust. I'm very hurt and disappointed. And then he looked at me, and he asked me a question. He said, Itkalo, was wearing the knee socks worth it to become a liar and to hurt your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to cry so bitterly. My father was a very good man. And I begged forgiveness. He picked me up. He gave me a hug. And he said, Itkalo, [NON-ENGLISH]. Every person makes a mistake, but only a fool makes twice the same. So remember it. And when I was in Auschwitz, I thought of it, how much honesty is important because we were betrayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from those little stories-- you know, they might seem so insignificant-- I remembered how important justice and honesty is. In there, I saw everything the reverse. And I felt trapped. We couldn't do anything. But we were trying to help each other, was [INAUDIBLE] because we realized that in extremity, this is the only way we survive, only together. You cannot survive alone. No one can survive in total isolation, and under those conditions from day to day, beating, and, and, and-- they made these infections.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked on this [INAUDIBLE] kommando for many-- for a long time. In 1942, which looking back, it was a sheer moment of luck, I was asked to-- I was taken to work of another place. I didn't know what it is. And it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. The reason they called it [NON-ENGLISH] is that all the girls were wearing a red kerchiefs. You know, we were bald. And we were, we were working-- we had to also work, go to work. Our work was from the clothes that when they took away all the bundles from, from the, from the people who came, those, that clothes was selected and sent to Germany, you know. So we had to sort it. That's when we could sabotage.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this that you started working?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1942. 1944, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think must have been in the later part of the year. The months, exactly, I don't know. I didn't have a calendar. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cold. That's why I assume it must have been in the wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at all when any of the Jewish holidays were, by the way, when you were in camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, personally, no. I had nobody to tell me. I didn't. But what I did is the prayers that I remembered by heart, I would say prayers, sometimes even they were unseasonable. But just to feel contact with, with my faith, you know. And there--

speaker: interviewer
text: What block were you in, by the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first block when I came was, was a, a woman from Czechoslovakia, and her name was Olga on block 8. Later, when I was in the [NON-ENGLISH], I was in another block. I no longer remember the name of it. But this [INAUDIBLE] I worked to the end. And-- so when we worked there, if we could, we used, you know, to damage some of the clothes because we knew that this going to go for the, for the Germans, you know. And it was very humiliating to work there because it's very hard for me to talk about it. But I will tell you. As I said, I was brought up very modest. We knew very little about sex. Nobody told us at home about sex or anything else. I never was-- till I was married, I was never sexually active. I didn't know anything about sex. I was never a normal teenager. My teenage years was in the concentration camps. In the ghetto, I was deprived of going to school, of, of going to proms, all the things that normal teenagers do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we would leave, they'd search us inwardly. If we didn't, because sometimes you would find in the clothes something. So they want to make sure that we didn't smuggle out any, any gold or anything else. And each time they made this examination, I feel like I'm dying inside. I felt so humiliated, so-- but what helped me a lot under those moments, believe it or not, I remembered my mother's words when I had the encounter. Itkalo, your menschlichkeit does not depend on how others treat you, but on how you treat others. And I would say, well, I'm not doing it to them, they're doing it to me. So this way, I didn't lose my self-worth, you know. It was painful, emotionally very painful a lot of times. So working-- Were people able to smuggle stuff out of Kanada or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hardly believe so because they searched us. They searched us in, in private places. Can you imagine the humiliation and pain? So it was-- one thing that was good though that was our advantage, was both painful and was to our advantage, is that because it was [NON-ENGLISH] Hitler said that they are the superior race, and we're the inferior. And the Gypsies, too, they said they inferior. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with Gypsies?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Gypsies, but not in the same-- the Jewish people, the Jewish girls were separate. I was never with men. I was never with any other group. There were other groups, too. There were Jehovah Witness. There were Gypsies. There were political. There were homosexuals. But we were not together with them. Every group had different, different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because of the [INAUDIBLE] that-- I don't know. It's just so overwhelming when I start thinking about all, all the horrible thing. And one of the things which also-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whenever I found out that they-- see, they had a live orchestra. Jewish people played live orchestra. And whenever a transport came, a new transport arrived and they were making the selection and burning people, the music played. Many times-- for a long time, when I would hear music, I started to cry. People wouldn't understand why. And I love music. You can see I have lots of tapes and things, but-- because I always remembered the barbarism. Can you imagine killing people and music playing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And with Mengele and with other nurses who were giving injection for Jew because it wasn't always the gas chambers. I was told, this I didn't experience, because when I came, unfortunately they had the gas chambers. But before, they used the gas vents. They gave injections, you know, in, in the heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so the point is that when I think to myself, this was the most educated people, professors, college graduates. So what was wrong? And I was told that education, the importance of education, the People of the Book from, from-- my, my great grandfather was a scholar of, of-- my, my grandmother, my grandparents. And here, what did they do with the books? We didn't have electricity. I had to go in with a candle down to the root cellar, but I had the love and the support. So that's when I started to compare the values with everything. And I realized once something very important, very important-- that everybody is human. But not everybody's humane. And that menchlichkeit is the highest form of religion, education, and achievement because without it, everything fails. Knowledge is never wrong in itself, it's how you use it. So I didn't stop craving knowledge. But I started to-- I said, how do I know people now? How can I tell? If doctors could do things like this, Mengele. And then, when I wrote a poem, which I'm going to share, is, 'I know you by the way you treat me, and by the way you make me feel inside. Those things tell me a lot about you. Those things, my friend, you cannot hide. It's not your color, your race, your religion, or occupation that tells me who you are. It's your deeds of kindness or of cruelty that manifests best who you are.' So that's the only criteria. That's the only criteria by which I know people. Nowadays, I have people who are black and friends who are black and white and Gentile and Jewish. I don't-- this is secondary. I-- when someone tells me, I have a degree, you don't impress me. I'm rich. You don't impress me. But your actions, your actions. And I realize this is important. This is the values observed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And under those conditions, we lived constantly, and hanging, and beating, and, and frustration, and constantly living in constant terror and fear for your life, and this constantly starving. For, for all those years, I was starved, tortured, and still wanted to survive. And under those conditions, till 1942-- from 1942 to 1945, I lived in Auschwitz. And then one morning we got up, and there was no appell, no counting, anymore. And we were taken on the death march.
